title: Complete iptables Tutorial: Beginner to Advanced
description: Master the Linux firewall from basics to advanced configurations. Learn to protect your server with practical, hands-on examples.
author: E-Learning Platform

sections.title: Introduction
content: Every server connected to a network needs protection. A firewall is your first line of defense — it controls which traffic is allowed in and out of your system. On Linux, the most widely used firewall tool is iptables.

This tutorial takes you from zero knowledge to advanced iptables usage. Each concept is explained clearly with copy-paste-ready commands. Whether you're securing a personal server or managing enterprise infrastructure, you'll find everything you need here.

infoBox.content: Prerequisites: You need a Linux system with sudo/root access. All commands shown use sudo — meaning they require administrator privileges.

sections.title: What is iptables?
content: iptables is a command-line utility that configures the built-in Linux kernel firewall (powered by the Netfilter framework). It lets you define rules that decide what happens to every network packet that passes through your system.

Think of iptables as a security checkpoint at an airport. Every packet (traveler) goes through a series of checks (rules). Based on those checks, the packet is either allowed through, sent away, or flagged for inspection.

cards.title: Tables
cards.desc: Groups of rules for specific purposes (filter, nat, mangle, raw)

cards.title: Chains
cards.desc: Ordered lists of rules (INPUT, OUTPUT, FORWARD, PREROUTING, POSTROUTING)

cards.title: Rules
cards.desc: Conditions that packets must match (IP, port, protocol, interface)

cards.title: Targets
cards.desc: Actions taken when a rule matches (ACCEPT, DROP, REJECT, LOG)

sections.title: How iptables Works
content: When a packet arrives at your system, the kernel sends it through iptables. The packet travels through tables and chains in a specific order:
packetFlow: 1. Packet arrives → PREROUTING chain (raw, mangle, nat tables)
2. Routing decision: Is this packet for this machine?
3a. Yes → INPUT chain (mangle, filter tables) → Local process
3b. No → FORWARD chain (mangle, filter tables) → POSTROUTING → Out
4. Local process sends packet → OUTPUT chain → POSTROUTING → Out

rows: Network Address Translation for routing packets
Modifies IP header properties (TTL, TOS, etc.)
Silently discards the packet (sender gets no response)
Discards the packet and sends an error back to sender
Records packet info to syslog (then continues to next rule)
Changes source IP for dynamically assigned addresses (NAT)

sections.title: Installation
content: iptables comes pre-installed on most Linux distributions. Let's verify and install if needed.

terminals.explanation: If you see a version number, iptables is installed. The (nf_tables) means it's using the modern nftables backend.
Installs iptables using the APT package manager.
This package saves your rules so they survive a reboot. During installation, it will ask whether to save current IPv4 and IPv6 rules — say Yes.
Enables the service to automatically restore rules on boot.

sections.title: Syntax & Options
content: Every iptables command follows this basic structure:

syntaxTerminal.explanation: This is the universal formula. Let's break down each part.

rows: Add a rule to the end of a chain
Insert a rule at a specific position (default: top)
Remove a rule from a chain
Show all rules in a chain
Remove all rules from a chain
Set the default policy for a chain

sections.title: View Current Rules
content: Before making changes, always check what rules already exist.

terminals.output:
Chain INPUT (policy ACCEPT)
target     prot opt source               destination

Chain FORWARD (policy ACCEPT)
target     prot opt source               destination

Chain OUTPUT (policy ACCEPT)
target     prot opt source               destination
terminals.explanation: This shows all three default chains in the filter table. 'policy ACCEPT' means if no rule matches, the packet is allowed through.

terminals.output:
Chain INPUT (policy ACCEPT 0 packets, 0 bytes)
 pkts bytes target     prot opt in     out     source               destination
  1234  123K ACCEPT     all  --  lo     any     anywhere             anywhere
   567 45678 ACCEPT     tcp  --  any    any     anywhere             anywhere             tcp dpt:ssh
terminals.explanation: The -v flag adds verbose output showing packet counts, byte counts, and interface information.

terminals.output:
Chain INPUT (policy ACCEPT)
num  target     prot opt source               destination
1    ACCEPT     all  --  anywhere             anywhere
2    ACCEPT     tcp  --  anywhere             anywhere             tcp dpt:ssh
3    ACCEPT     tcp  --  anywhere             anywhere             tcp dpt:http
terminals.explanation: Line numbers are essential when you want to delete or insert rules at specific positions.

terminals.output:
-P INPUT ACCEPT
-P FORWARD ACCEPT
-P OUTPUT ACCEPT
-A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp -m tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp -m tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
terminals.explanation: The -S flag shows rules in the exact command format used to create them. This is great for understanding and copying rules.

sections.title: Enable Loopback Traffic
content: The loopback interface (lo) is how your system talks to itself. Many services (databases, local APIs) rely on it. Always allow loopback traffic first.

terminals.explanation: Allows all incoming traffic on the loopback interface. The -i lo flag matches packets coming from the lo interface.
Allows all outgoing traffic on the loopback interface.

infoBox.content: Why this matters: If you block loopback, services like MySQL connecting via localhost (127.0.0.1) will stop working. Always add this rule before anything else.

sections.title: Allow Traffic for Services
content: The most common task is allowing traffic on specific ports for services your server runs.

terminals.explanation: -p tcp specifies the TCP protocol. --dport 80 matches packets destined for port 80. -j ACCEPT allows them through.
Same as HTTP but for encrypted HTTPS traffic on port 443.
Critical for remote server management. Add this BEFORE setting restrictive default policies!

subsections.title: Allow Both HTTP and HTTPS Together

terminals.command: sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp -m multiport --dports 80,443 -j ACCEPT
terminals.explanation: The multiport module lets you match multiple ports in a single rule, keeping your ruleset cleaner.

terminals.command: sudo iptables -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
terminals.explanation: This is crucial! It allows return traffic for connections your server initiated. Without this, your server can send requests but never receive responses.

infoBox.content: Important: If you're connected to a remote server via SSH, always allow SSH traffic before changing default policies. Otherwise you'll lock yourself out!

sections.title: Control Traffic by IP
content: You can allow or block traffic from specific IP addresses or entire subnets.

terminals.explanation: The -s flag specifies the source IP. All packets from 192.168.1.100 will be accepted.
DROP silently discards packets — the sender gets no response and will eventually time out.
REJECT sends back a 'connection refused' error, so the sender knows immediately that the connection was denied.
The /24 CIDR notation blocks the entire range from 203.0.113.0 to 203.0.113.255 (256 addresses).

subsections.title: Allow SSH Only from a Specific IP

terminals.explanation: This is a security best practice — restrict SSH access to only your trusted IP address.

subsections.title: Block Traffic to a Specific Interface

terminals.explanation: Blocks traffic from 203.0.113.51 only when it arrives on the eth0 interface.

sections.title: Log Dropped Packets
content: Logging helps you understand what traffic is being blocked and is invaluable for troubleshooting.

terminals.explanation: The LOG target records packet information to syslog. The --log-prefix adds a custom tag to make log entries easy to find.
After logging, drop the packet. Note: LOG doesn't stop processing — the packet continues to the next rule.
dmesg shows kernel messages. grep filters for our custom prefix.
Alternative: watch syslog in real-time for dropped packets. Use Ctrl+C to stop.

infoBox.content: Pro tip: Be careful with logging — on a busy server, logging every dropped packet can fill up disk space quickly. Use it for debugging, then remove or limit it in production.

sections.title: INPUT, OUTPUT & FORWARD Chains Deep Dive
content: Understanding the three main chains in the filter table is essential. Each chain handles packets at a different stage of their journey through your system.

subsections.title: INPUT Chain — Incoming Traffic to Your Machine
subsections.content: The INPUT chain handles packets destined for local processes on your machine. When someone connects to your web server, SSH, or any service running locally, the packet passes through the INPUT chain.

terminals.explanation: Allow incoming web traffic on port 80. This rule is evaluated in the INPUT chain.
Block all traffic from 10.0.0.5 to any local service.
Allow localhost traffic — essential for local services like databases.

subsections.contentAfter: Packet flow: Packet arrives → Routing decision → Destination is THIS machine → INPUT chain → Local process

subsections.title: OUTPUT Chain — Outgoing Traffic from Your Machine
subsections.content: The OUTPUT chain handles packets generated by local processes on your machine. When your server makes an HTTP request, sends email, or downloads updates, the packet passes through the OUTPUT chain.

terminals.explanation: Allow outgoing HTTPS traffic. Your server can fetch updates or call APIs.
Block outgoing SMTP to prevent your server from being used as a spam relay.
Allow outgoing localhost traffic.

subsections.contentAfter: Packet flow: Local process sends packet → OUTPUT chain → Routing decision → POSTROUTING → Out

subsections.title: FORWARD Chain — Traffic Passing Through Your Machine
subsections.content: The FORWARD chain handles packets that pass THROUGH your system — not destined for it, not generated by it. This is used when your machine acts as a router, gateway, or firewall between two networks.

terminals.command: sudo iptables -A FORWARD -i eth1 -o eth0 -j ACCEPT
terminals.explanation: Allow traffic from internal network (eth1) to external network (eth0).

terminals.command: sudo iptables -A FORWARD -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
terminals.explanation: Allow return traffic for forwarded connections — essential for two-way communication.
Block all forwarding. This is the default for machines that are NOT routers.

subsections.contentAfter: Packet flow: Packet arrives → Routing decision → Destination is ANOTHER machine → FORWARD chain → POSTROUTING → Out

infoBox.content: Key insight: INPUT and OUTPUT are for traffic to/from YOUR machine. FORWARD is for traffic passing THROUGH your machine (router/gateway mode). Most servers only need INPUT and OUTPUT rules.

sections.title: Moving Rules: Top to Bottom & Bottom to Top
content: Rule order is critical in iptables. Sometimes you need to reorder existing rules without deleting and recreating them.

subsections.title: Understanding Rule Order
subsections.content: iptables evaluates rules from top (position 1) to bottom. The first matching rule wins and stops further evaluation. This means a DROP rule at position 1 will block traffic before an ACCEPT rule at position 5 ever gets checked.

terminals.output:
Chain INPUT (policy ACCEPT)
num  target     prot opt source               destination
1    ACCEPT     all  --  anywhere             anywhere
2    ACCEPT     tcp  --  anywhere             anywhere             tcp dpt:ssh
3    ACCEPT     tcp  --  anywhere             anywhere             tcp dpt:http
4    DROP       all  --  10.0.0.99            anywhere
terminals.explanation: Notice rule 4 drops traffic from 10.0.0.99. But what if we want it at position 2 to be evaluated earlier?

subsections.title: Move a Rule to the TOP (Position 1)
subsections.content: The easiest way to move a rule to the top is to insert a copy at position 1, then delete the old one:

terminals.explanation: -I INPUT 1 inserts at position 1. All existing rules shift down by one.

terminals.output:
Chain INPUT (policy ACCEPT)
num  target     prot opt source               destination
1    DROP       all  --  10.0.0.99            anywhere
2    ACCEPT     all  --  anywhere             anywhere
3    ACCEPT     tcp  --  anywhere             anywhere             tcp dpt:ssh
4    ACCEPT     tcp  --  anywhere             anywhere             tcp dpt:http
5    DROP       all  --  10.0.0.99            anywhere
terminals.explanation: Now the DROP rule is at position 1. The old copy is still at position 5.
Delete the old duplicate at position 5. Note: after the insert, the old rule shifted from 4 to 5.

subsections.title: Move a Rule to a Specific Position

terminals.explanation: Insert a copy at position 3. Rules 3 and 4 shift down.
Delete the old copy (now at position 6 after the insert shifted everything).

subsections.title: Move a Rule to the BOTTOM
subsections.content: To move a rule to the bottom, insert it at the end using -A (append) and delete the old one:

terminals.explanation: -A appends to the end of the chain.
Delete the old copy at position 1.

subsections.title: Swap Two Rules
subsections.content: To swap rule 2 and rule 3, insert rule 3's content at position 2, then delete the old position 4:

terminals.explanation: Insert HTTPS rule at position 2. This pushes the old rule 2 down.
Delete the old HTTPS rule (now at position 4 after the insert).

subsections.title: One-Liner: Move Rule Using Insert + Delete
subsections.content: Combine insert and delete in one line for efficiency:

terminals.command: sudo iptables -I INPUT 1 -s 10.0.0.99 -j DROP && sudo iptables -D INPUT 5
terminals.explanation: Inserts at position 1, then deletes the old copy at position 5. Adjust the delete number based on the new position after insert.

infoBox.content: Important: When you insert a rule, ALL rules at that position and below shift down by one. Always list rules with --line-numbers before and after to confirm the correct positions.

sections.title: Delete Rules
content: There are several ways to remove rules depending on what you need.

subsections.title: Delete by Line Number
subsections.content: First, list rules with line numbers:

terminals.output:
Chain INPUT (policy ACCEPT)
num  target     prot opt source               destination
1    ACCEPT     all  --  anywhere             anywhere
2    ACCEPT     tcp  --  anywhere             anywhere             tcp dpt:ssh
3    ACCEPT     tcp  --  anywhere             anywhere             tcp dpt:http
4    DROP       all  --  203.0.113.51         anywhere

subsections.contentAfter: Then delete the rule you want (e.g., rule 4):

terminalsAfter.explanation: -D INPUT 4 deletes rule number 4 from the INPUT chain. The remaining rules shift up automatically.

terminals.explanation: This deletes the exact rule that drops traffic from 203.0.113.51. You must specify the rule exactly as it was created.
Removes ALL rules from all chains. Your default policies (ACCEPT/DROP) remain unchanged.
Deletes all user-defined (custom) chains. Use this after -F to completely reset.

terminals.command: sudo iptables -P INPUT ACCEPT && sudo iptables -P FORWARD ACCEPT && sudo iptables -P OUTPUT ACCEPT
terminals.explanation: Resets all default policies to ACCEPT. Combined with -F and -X, this gives you a completely clean slate.

infoBox.content: Warning: Running -F on a remote server with a DROP policy will immediately disconnect you! Always set the policy to ACCEPT first, or have console access ready.

sections.title: Block All Except SSH
content: This is a common security setup: deny everything by default, then explicitly allow what you need.

infoBox.content: Critical: Execute these commands in order. If you set the DROP policy before allowing SSH, you will be locked out of your server!

terminals.command: sudo iptables -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

subsections.title: Step 4: Set Default Policy to DROP

terminals.explanation: Now any incoming packet that doesn't match one of the rules above will be dropped. SSH still works because we added that rule first.

terminals.output:
Chain INPUT (policy DROP)
 pkts bytes target     prot opt in     out     source               destination
  500  50000 ACCEPT     all  --  lo     any     anywhere             anywhere
  300  30000 ACCEPT     all  --  any    any     anywhere             anywhere             ctstate RELATED,ESTABLISHED
   50   5000 ACCEPT     tcp  --  any    any     anywhere             anywhere             tcp dpt:ssh

Chain FORWARD (policy ACCEPT)
 pkts bytes target     prot opt in     out     source               destination

Chain OUTPUT (policy ACCEPT)
 pkts bytes target     prot opt in     out     source               destination
terminals.explanation: Notice the INPUT policy is now DROP. Only loopback, established connections, and SSH are allowed.

sections.title: Save Your Changes
content: iptables rules are not persistent by default. If you reboot your server, all rules will be lost. You must save them explicitly.

terminals.explanation: Saves current rules to /etc/iptables/rules.v4. Rules will be automatically restored on boot.
Saves rules to /etc/sysconfig/iptables.
Exports all current rules to a file. You can name and place this file anywhere.
Restores rules from a saved file. Useful for applying backup configurations.

infoBox.content: Best practice: After saving, reboot your server and run sudo iptables -L to verify rules were restored correctly.

sections.title: NAT & Port Forwarding
content: NAT (Network Address Translation) lets you redirect traffic between networks or forward ports to internal servers.

subsections.title: Enable IP Forwarding
subsections.content: Before NAT works, the kernel must be allowed to forward packets:

terminals.explanation: Enables IP forwarding immediately. To make it permanent, add net.ipv4.ip_forward=1 to /etc/sysctl.conf

subsections.title: MASQUERADE (Share Internet Connection)
subsections.content: Allow devices on a private network to access the internet through your server:

terminals.command: sudo iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE
terminals.explanation: -t nat uses the NAT table. POSTROUTING modifies packets after routing. MASQUERADE replaces the source IP with the server's external IP.

subsections.title: Port Forwarding (DNAT)
subsections.content: Forward incoming traffic on port 80 to an internal server at 10.0.0.5:

terminals.command: sudo iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -i eth0 -p tcp --dport 80 -j DNAT --to-destination 10.0.0.5:80
terminals.explanation: PREROUTING modifies packets before routing. DNAT changes the destination address to 10.0.0.5.

subsections.contentAfter: Allow the forwarded traffic through the filter table:

terminals.explanation: Shows all NAT table rules with verbose output. The -n flag prevents DNS lookups for faster display.

subsections.title: Rate Limiting (Prevent Brute Force)
subsections.content: Limit SSH connection attempts to prevent brute force attacks:

terminals.command: sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -m conntrack --ctstate NEW -m recent --set --name SSH
sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -m conntrack --ctstate NEW -m recent --update --seconds 60 --hitcount 4 --name SSH -j DROP
terminals.explanation: Drops new SSH connections if the same IP has made more than 4 attempts in 60 seconds.

terminals.command: sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --syn --dport 80 -m connlimit --connlimit-above 20 -j DROP
terminals.explanation: Drops connections from any IP that has more than 20 simultaneous connections to port 80. Protects against DDoS.

terminals.command: sudo iptables -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate INVALID -j DROP
terminals.explanation: Drops packets that don't belong to any known connection. These are often malformed or malicious.

terminals.command: sudo iptables -A INPUT -p icmp --icmp-type echo-request -j ACCEPT
terminals.explanation: Allows ping requests. You can also rate-limit ping:

terminals.command: sudo iptables -A INPUT -p icmp --icmp-type echo-request -m limit --limit 1/s --limit-burst 4 -j ACCEPT
terminals.explanation: Allows ping at 1 packet per second with a burst of 4. Excess pings are dropped.

subsections.title: Custom Chains
subsections.content: Create your own chains to organize complex rulesets:

terminals.explanation: Creates a new chain called WEB_SERVICES.
Jumps to the WEB_SERVICES chain from INPUT. This keeps your main chain clean and organized.
If your server shouldn't send email, block outgoing SMTP to prevent it from being used as a spam relay.

subsections.title: Rule Order Matters
subsections.content: iptables evaluates rules from top to bottom. The first matching rule wins. A broad DROP rule placed too early can block traffic before a specific allow rule is ever reached.

terminals.explanation: Always check rule order when something isn't working as expected.

subsections.title: Insert Rules at a Specific Position

terminals.explanation: -I INPUT 2 inserts the rule at position 2, pushing existing rules down. Use this when you need a rule to be evaluated before others.
Returns exit code 0 if the rule exists, 1 if it doesn't. Useful in scripts.

subsections.title: Locked Out of Your Server?
subsections.content: If you blocked SSH by mistake:
steps: Use your hosting provider's web console or recovery console to access the server
Set policy to ACCEPT: sudo iptables -P INPUT ACCEPT

infoBox.content: IPv6 Warning: iptables only handles IPv4. If your server has IPv6 enabled, you must also configure ip6tables separately, or traffic may bypass your firewall over IPv6.

rows: sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport PORT -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

infoBox.content: Remember: Always test your firewall rules in a non-production environment first. Keep a second SSH session open when making changes to remote servers so you don't lock yourself out.
